attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- statusline: Set up Claude Code's status line UI
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch
- insights: Generate a report analyzing your Claude Code sessions
- team-onboarding: Help teammates ramp on Claude Code with a guide from your usage

# Transform Dialect

Fine-grain transformation control dialect. See [tutorial](../Tutorials/transform) for more introductory information.

[TOC]

## Overview

This dialect provides operations that can be used to control transformation
of the IR using a different portion of the IR. It refers to the IR being
transformed as payload IR, and to the IR guiding the transformation as
transform IR.

The main use case for this dialect is orchestrating fine-grain transformations
on individual IR objects (operations or values) or sets thereof. For example, it
may involve finding loop-like operations with specific properties (e.g., large
size) in the payload IR, applying loop tiling to those and only those
operations, and then applying loop unrolling to the inner loops produced by the
previous transformations. As such, it is not intended as a replacement for the
pass infrastructure, nor for the pattern rewriting infrastructure. In the most
common case, the transform IR will be processed and applied to the payload IR by
a pass. Transformations expressed by the Transform dialect may be implemented
using the pattern infrastructure or any other relevant MLIR component.

The following IR gives a rough idea of what the operations in this dialect
may look like without using actually existing operations:

```mlir
%0 = transform.loop.find { size > 42 } : !transform.interface<tileable>
%1 = transform.compute_trailing_tile_size %0 : !transform.param<index>
%2:2 = transform.loop.tile %0 tile_sizes(1, 4, %1)
      : (!transform.interface<tileable>)
     -> (!transform.op<loop>, !transform.op<loop>)
%3 = transform.get_op_result [0] %2#0 : !transform.any_value
transform.assign_to_fast_memory %3
transform.loop.unroll %1#1 : !transform.op<loop>
```

The values used in the Transform dialect may correspond to:

  * sets of operations in the payload IR;

  * sets of values in the payload IR;

  * sets of parameters (attributes) known at the execution time of the
    transform dialect.

The former two kinds of values are also referred to as operation and value
*handles*, respectively. In the example above, `%0` corresponds to the set of
loops found in the payload IR that satisfy the condition, and `%2` correspond to
groups of outer and inner loops, respectively, produced by the tiling
transformation. `%3` corresponds to a set of values that are produced by the
outer loops after tiling. `%1` corresponds to a list of tile sizes selected for
each of the operations that `%0` corresponds to.

An operation handle such as `%0` may be associated with multiple payload
operations. This is conceptually a set of operations and no assumptions should
be made about the order of ops unless specified otherwise by the operation.
Similarly, a value handle such as `%3` may be associated with a set of payload
IR values. Transform dialect operations may take as operands and produce an
arbitrary combination of values representing handles and parameters. Most
Transform IR ops support operand values that are mapped to multiple payload
objects. They usually apply the respective transformation for every mapped
object ("batched execution"). Deviations from this convention are described in
the documentation of Transform IR ops.

Parameters, such as `%1` in the above example, have two logical roles in
transform IR. In parameter based control, they carry the values needed to
execute the explicit control defined by the transforms, for example:

```mlir
%0 = transform.match.structured.rank %linalg_op_handle : !transform.param<index>
%1 = transform.param.constant 3 : i32 -> !transform.param<index>
transform.execute_if_cmpi eq %0, %1 : !transform.param<index>, !transform.param<index>
// Some nested body of transform ops
```

Alternatively, parameters can associate with the payload IR where the specific
value at execution time has no bearing on the execution of the transform IR. In
other words, parameters can either associate with the transform IR or the

# Transform Dialect Tutorial

MLIR supports declarative specification for controlling compiler transformations
via the transform dialect. It allows one to request compiler transformations
using compiler IR itself, which can be embedded into the original IR that is

=== Ch0.md ===
# Chapter 0: A Primer on “Structured” Linalg Operations

Before starting the tutorial on the Transform dialect, let us take a brief look at the concept of Structured operations and its implementation in the Linalg dialect. Note that the Transform dialect does not require Structured operations and vice versa. The two co-evolved at the beginning of the Transform dialect, which makes the subset of transformations for Structured operations the most mature and most suitable for the tutorial. If you are already familiar with this concept, skip to Chapter 1.

Structured code generation intends to preserve the structure of the computation for as long as necessary to enable transformations, up to and including the design of IR abstractions that support specific transformations.

=== Ch1.md ===
# Chapter 1: Combining Existing Transformations

## Introduction

The Transform dialect allows one to precisely target transformations at specific operations in the IR and to chain them, that is to apply a transformation to operations produced by the previous transformation. To achieve this, transformations are expressed as other operations in the IR. We call these the IR containing these operations transform IR. And we call the IR that is being transformed payload IR.

=== Ch2.md ===
# Chapter 2: Adding a Simple New Transformation Operation

## Setting Up to Add New Transformations

Before defining a new transform operation, we need to choose where its implementation should be located. While MLIR encourages upstream contributions, it is not always possible or even desirable to modify the main Transform dialect, for example, if the transformation is specific to some out-of-tree dialect that is not itself available upstream.

=== Ch3.md ===
# Chapter 3: More than Simple Transform Operations

## Type Constraints and ApplyEach Trait

A transform operation that applies to each payload operation individually and requires it to be of a specific kind is a repeated pattern. One can use Transform dialect types to specify the preconditions of the type. Specifically, we can change the expected operand type from the wide `TransformHandleTypeInterface` to the more narrow `Transform_ConcreteOp<"func.call">`. Furthermore, we use the `TransformEachOpTrait` trait to provide the skeleton implementation of the `apply` method that performs verification, iteration over payloads and result concatenation. The improved ODS op definition is as follows.

[mlir/test/Examples/transform/Ch4](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/mlir/test/Examples/transform/Ch4).**


=== ChH.md ===
# Chapter H: Reproducing Halide Schedule

This chapter demonstrates how a schedule from the [Halide
DSL](http://halide-lang.org) can be implemented using Transform dialect for
structured ops.

# MLIR Transform Dialect - Reading List

How to write Transform dialect scripts to transform MLIR IR.
All paths relative to `mlir/`.

---

## 1. Documentation

### Dialect Overview (480 lines)

- `docs/Dialects/Transform.md` - Core concepts: payload vs transform IR, operation/value handles, parameters, type system, interpreter semantics, error handling model

### Tutorial Series (2692 lines total)

Read in order:

| File | Title | What You Learn |
|------|-------|----------------|
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/_index.md` | Index | Overview of the tutorial series |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch0.md` | Structured Linalg Primer | Structured ops concept in Linalg dialect (prerequisite context) |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch1.md` | Combining Existing Transformations | Chaining transforms, targeting specific ops, handle invalidation |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch2.md` | Adding a Simple New Transformation Op | Where to place new ops, ODS definition, implementing `apply` |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch3.md` | More than Simple Transform Ops | Type constraints, `TransformEachOpTrait`, matcher ops |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch4.md` | Matching Payload with Transform Ops | Pattern matching payload IR, `foreach_match`, composable matchers |
| `docs/Tutorials/transform/ChH.md` | Reproducing Halide Schedule | End-to-end example: tiling, vectorization, parallelization a la Halide |

---

## 2. Op Definitions (.td) - User-Facing API Reference

These TableGen files define all available transform operations with their descriptions, operands, results, and usage examples.

### Core Ops

| File | Lines | Content |
|------|-------|---------|
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IR/TransformOps.td` | 1383 | All core transform ops (sequence, foreach, match, apply, cast, etc.) |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IR/TransformTypes.td` | 106 | Handle types: `!transform.any_op`, `!transform.any_value`, `!transform.param` |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IR/TransformAttrs.td` | 36 | Transform dialect attributes |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IR/TransformDialect.td` | 169 | Dialect definition and base classes |

### Interfaces

| File | Lines | Content |
|------|-------|---------|
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/Interfaces/TransformInterfaces.td` | 413 | `TransformOpInterface`, traits for building custom ops |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/Interfaces/MatchInterfaces.td` | 66 | Interfaces for matcher operations |

### Extension Ops

| File | Lines | Content |
|------|-------|---------|
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/LoopExtension/LoopExtensionOps.td` | 76 | Loop unrolling transforms |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/PDLExtension/PDLExtensionOps.td` | 104 | PDL-based pattern matching integration |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/DebugExtension/DebugExtensionOps.td` | 67 | Debug/print ops for transform scripts |
| `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IRDLExtension/IRDLExtensionOps.td` | 36 | IRDL dialect constraint integration |

---

## 3. Test Cases - Usage Examples (6173 lines total)

### Basic Op Syntax

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/ops.mlir` | Valid syntax for all core transform ops |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/ops-invalid.mlir` | Invalid syntax and expected error messages |

### Interpreter and Execution

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/interpreter.mlir` | Core interpreter execution |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-interpreter.mlir` | Comprehensive interpreter behavior |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-interpreter-printing.mlir` | Output formatting during transform execution |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-interpreter-debug.mlir` | Debug mode with `debug-payload-root-tag` |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/interpreter-entry-point.mlir` | Custom entry point via `entry-point=` option |

### Targeting and Matching

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/selective-targeting.mlir` | Targeting specific operations (e.g., matmul) for transformation |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/foreach-match.mlir` | `foreach_match` pattern for finding and transforming ops |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/apply-foreach-nested.mlir` | Nested foreach application |

### Multi-Argument Binding

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/multi-arg-top-level-ops.mlir` | Binding trailing args to ops via `debug-bind-trailing-args` |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/multi-arg-top-level-params.mlir` | Binding trailing args as parameters |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/multi-arg-top-level-values.mlir` | Binding trailing args as values |

### Pattern and Pass Application

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-pattern-application.mlir` | Applying rewrite patterns from transform scripts |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-pass-application.mlir` | Applying passes from transform scripts |

### External Libraries and Modules

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-interpreter-external.mlir` | Loading external transform definitions |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-interpreter-external-concurrent.mlir` | Concurrent external module loading |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/preload-library.mlir` | Preloading transform libraries |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/preload-library-invalid.mlir` | Invalid library loading scenarios |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/include/test-interpreter-external-source.mlir` | External source definitions (included by tests) |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/include/test-interpreter-external-concurrent-source.mlir` | Concurrent source definitions |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/include/test-interpreter-library/definitions-self-contained.mlir` | Self-contained library definitions |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/include/test-interpreter-library/definitions-with-unresolved.mlir` | Library with unresolved symbols |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/include/Library/lower-to-llvm.mlir` | Example library: lowering to LLVM |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/Library/lower-to-llvm-transform-symbol-def.mlir` | Library symbol definition example |

### Extension Tests

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-pdl-extension.mlir` | PDL pattern matching from transform scripts |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-loop-transforms.mlir` | Loop transformations (unroll, etc.) |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/irdl.mlir` | IRDL constraint usage in transforms |

### State and Safety

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/transform-state-extension.mlir` | Transform state management |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/transform-state-extension-initializer.mlir` | State extension initialization |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/check-use-after-free.mlir` | Handle invalidation and use-after-free detection |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/expensive-checks.mlir` | Expensive validation checks |
| `test/Dialect/Transform/infer-effects.mlir` | Effect inference on transform ops |

### Dialect Injection

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Dialect/Transform/test-dialect-injection.mlir` | Registering dialect extensions |

### Tutorial Examples (continuously tested)

| File | What It Demonstrates |
|------|---------------------|
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch1/sequence.mlir` | Ch1: chaining tile + interchange on linalg ops |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch1/invalidation-1.mlir` | Ch1: handle invalidation example 1 |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch1/invalidation-2.mlir` | Ch1: handle invalidation example 2 |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch2/sequence.mlir` | Ch2: using a custom transform op |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch2/ops.mlir` | Ch2: custom op definition test |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch2/invalid.mlir` | Ch2: invalid custom op usage |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch3/sequence.mlir` | Ch3: type-constrained ops with TransformEachOpTrait |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch3/ops.mlir` | Ch3: refined op definitions |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch3/invalid.mlir` | Ch3: type constraint violations |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch4/sequence.mlir` | Ch4: matching and transforming payload |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch4/multiple.mlir` | Ch4: multiple match targets |
| `test/Examples/transform/Ch4/features.mlir` | Ch4: advanced matching features |
| `test/Examples/transform/ChH/full.mlir` | ChH: full Halide-style schedule reproduction |

---

## Suggested Reading Order

1. `docs/Dialects/Transform.md` - Understand the conceptual model
2. `docs/Tutorials/transform/Ch0.md` through `ChH.md` - Walk through the tutorial
3. `test/Examples/transform/Ch1/sequence.mlir` through `ChH/full.mlir` - Run the tutorial examples
4. `include/mlir/Dialect/Transform/IR/TransformOps.td` - Reference all available ops
5. `test/Dialect/Transform/selective-targeting.mlir` - Study real targeting patterns
6. `test/Dialect/Transform/foreach-match.mlir` - Study matching patterns
7. Extension .td files - Learn loop, PDL, debug, IRDL ops as needed
